attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with increasing shortness of breath and fatigue over six months. She immigrated from Latin America five years ago. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears tired. Her pulse rate is irregular at 95/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Cardiac examination reveals an irregular rhythm with a soft systolic murmur. Her jugular venous pressure is elevated and there is mild ankle oedema. An ECG shows right bundle branch block with frequent ventricular ectopics.

An echocardiogram reveals dilated cardiomyopathy with an ejection fraction of 35%. Serology testing is pending.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely implicated pathogen?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features of chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy, which typically develops in 30-40% of people infected with Trypanosoma cruzi decades after the initial infection. The diagnosis is supported by her origin from Latin America (an endemic area) and the characteristic cardiac manifestations including conduction abnormalities and dilated cardiomyopathy.

Trypanosoma cruzi is transmitted by triatomine bugs (kissing bugs) in Central and South America. After initial infection, many patients remain asymptomatic for decades before developing chronic complications, particularly cardiac involvement.

Leishmania donovani causes visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar), which typically presents with fever, hepatosplenomegaly, and pancytopenia. While it can affect the heart, this is not its primary manifestation.

Trypanosoma brucei causes African trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness) and is transmitted by tsetse flies in sub-Saharan Africa. It primarily affects the central nervous system rather than the heart.

Entamoeba histolytica causes amoebic dysentery and liver abscess. It does not cause cardiac complications.

Toxoplasma gondii can cause myocarditis in immunocompromised patients, but would not typically cause chronic dilated cardiomyopathy with conduction abnormalities in an immunocompetent person.
learningPoints: Chagas disease is caused by Trypanosoma cruzi transmitted by triatomine bugs in Central and South America.
Chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy presents with conduction abnormalities and dilated cardiomyopathy.
Diagnosis is supported by positive serology and typical cardiac manifestations in patients from endemic areas.
references: Rausch K, Lau K, Ulett K, et al. Orthotopic cardiac transplantation for Chagas cardiomyopathy in Australia. Intern Med J. 2019;49(9):1194-1195. doi:10.1111/imj.14421
Pinto A, Pett S, Jackson Y. Identifying Chagas disease in Australia: an emerging challenge for general practitioners. Aust Fam Physician. 2014;43(7):440-442.
